evidence_citations.excerpt: I keep pushing myself to the brink, but the anxiety keeps spiraling

transcript.text: I feel like I'm drowning in exams and my parents' ultimatum. Every time I think about turning in my work, I worry they'll see me as a failure. It's exhausting to keep trying to meet their expectations while also fighting for my identity. I just want a break.

You're dealing with a lot right now, from exams to your parents' ultimatum, and it's clear that the pressure to meet their expectations is really getting to you. It sounds like the fear of being seen as a failure is a heavy burden. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you didn't meet those expectations, and how that's affecting your daily life?

Honestly, if I don't meet their expectations, I feel like I'd lose my scholarship and they'd see me as a total failure. That thought makes me panic and I end up overworking, but I also feel worthless and like I'm not good enough. I keep pushing myself to the brink, but the anxiety keeps spiraling.

The thought of not meeting your parents' expectations and potentially losing your scholarship is really terrifying for you, and it's like that fear is always lurking, making you feel like you're not good enough. It's like you're trying to prove yourself to them and to yourself, but that's a really tough burden to carry. You're already shouldering so much with your exams and your identity, and it feels like this extra pressure is just overwhelming. Can you think of any times when you've felt like you were enough, regardless of your grades or your parents' expectations?

I guess I felt somewhat enough when I helped a friend at the LGBTQ center, but that was a small moment. Even then, I was still worried about doing it perfectly and that the effort would be enough. The weight of mom's ultimatum and the midterms just keep piling up.

It's really interesting that you mention feeling somewhat enough when you helped a friend at the LGBTQ center - that takes a lot of courage and empathy. Even though it was a small moment, it sounds like it was a significant one for you. Yeah, it's tough when the weight of your mom's ultimatum and midterms keeps piling up, making it hard to hold onto those positive feelings. Can you tell me more about what it was about helping your friend that made you feel more like enough?

It felt good to help, but I still couldn't shake the feeling that I was doing it to prove something to my mom, not just because I wanted to. That thought keeps me on edge, especially with midterms looming. I keep worrying I'm not good enough, even when I do something kind.

You're caught between wanting to do good and feeling like you need to prove yourself to your mom, which is a really tough spot to be in. It's like you're trying to reconcile your own desires with the pressure to meet expectations. Helping your friend at the LGBTQ center was a significant moment for you, but even that was tainted by the feeling that you were doing it to prove something. I can see how that would keep you on edge, especially with midterms looming. It sounds like you're struggling to shake off the feeling that you're not good enough, even when you do something kind.

I keep thinking that my help at the center was just a way to get my mom to accept me, not because I wanted it. It feels like every good thing I do is weighed against what she wants. I'm also scrambling to cram for midterms, and the more I study, the more I doubt I’m enough.

You're feeling really trapped between trying to meet your mom's expectations and wanting to do things for yourself, and that's making it hard for you to feel like you're doing anything genuinely. Even helping at the LGBTQ center, which sounds like a really positive experience, gets tainted by the feeling that you're just trying to prove yourself to your mom. With midterms coming up, that pressure is probably feeling even more overwhelming. It's like every good thing you do is being weighed against what she wants, and that's a really tough way to live.

I feel like I'm constantly on a treadmill that never stops, and every time I think I've caught up, there's another deadline or another "mom's expectation" to meet. The pressure to score high on midterms just adds to the guilt I feel for not being able to give myself a break. I keep telling myself I need to be perfect, but I'm just exhausted and terrified of failing everyone, especially my parents.

You're feeling exhausted and terrified of failing everyone, especially your parents, and that's a really heavy burden to carry. It's like every deadline or expectation is a reminder that you're not good enough, and that's taking a toll on your mental health. I can see how the pressure to score high on midterms is adding to that guilt, and it's making it hard for you to give yourself a break. What's it like for you to feel like you need to be perfect, and how is that affecting your daily life?

I keep telling myself I have to be perfect, and it’s exhausting—like I’m running on a treadmill that never stops. The pressure to nail my midterms feels like a personal failure if I don’t hit the marks, so I can’t even focus on studying. It’s hard to feel good about myself when every mistake feels like a sign I’m not enough.

The pressure to nail your midterms is feeling like a huge weight on your shoulders, and it's like every mistake is being magnified as a sign that you're not enough. You've been pushing yourself so hard to meet expectations, and it's taking a toll on your mental health. Can you think of any ways that you've been able to take a step back and focus on your own goals, rather than just trying to meet someone else's standards?

I did try setting a timer to break up my study sessions, but every time the timer goes off I just get a sharper reminder of how much I still have to cover. I'm still stuck thinking that if I don’t get a perfect score I’m nothing. It feels like the pressure is a constant loop that drains me.

You've been trying to break up your study sessions with a timer, but it's not giving you the relief you hoped for. Instead, it's just reminding you of how much you still have to cover, and that's really tough. It sounds like the pressure to perform is still feeling overwhelming, and it's hard to shake the feeling that if you don't get a perfect score, you're nothing. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when the timer goes off and you're reminded of how much you still have to do?

When the timer clicks, I feel the weight of all the grades I still need to get and start thinking about how my mom would judge me if I don’t hit that mark. It feels like a domino: one missed point means I’m failing not just the class but my whole identity. The anxiety spikes and I question whether I’m even good enough to keep going.

When that timer clicks, it's like the whole weight of your mom's expectations and your own desire to prove yourself comes crashing down, and it's hard to breathe. It feels like one missed point isn't just a missed point, but a reflection of your whole worth. I can see how that would make you question whether you're even good enough to keep going. Yeah, it's a really tough spot to be in. Does it feel like that pressure is always lurking, even when you're trying to focus on your own goals?

I think the pressure is always lurking, even when I try to focus on my own goals. It feels like I'm constantly on a treadmill, and every time I think I'm catching up, something else pulls me back. I keep worrying that if I don't hit the marks, I'm nothing, and that makes it hard to even breathe.

You're feeling like you're on a treadmill that never stops, and every time you think you're catching up, something else pulls you back. That must be really overwhelming. It's like the pressure to hit the marks is always lurking, making it hard to breathe and feel good about yourself. I can see how that would make you worry that if you don't meet those expectations, you're nothing. Yeah, that's a really tough spot to be in. Can you tell me more about what happens when you try to focus on your own goals, and how that feels compared to trying to meet someone else's standards?

turn_traces.user_message: I feel like I'm drowning in exams and my parents' ultimatum. Every time I think about turning in my work, I worry they'll see me as a failure. It's exhausting to keep trying to meet their expectations while also fighting for my identity. I just want a break.

common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission

common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here to support this person as they navigate their feelings of inadequacy and the pressure to be perfect, and it seems like they're really struggling to shake off the weight of their parents' expectations. Given their intent to resist and reflect on their emotions, I'll use strategies like support and complex reflection to help them process their feelings. The evidence from their recent turns suggests that they're feeling overwhelmed by the pressure to meet expectations and the fear of not being good enough, and that's affecting their ability to focus on their studies. My entry point will be to acknowledge the exhaustion and frustration they're feeling, and to help them explore ways to reframe their thinking around perfectionism.

evidence_used.content: feeling exhausted and terrified of failing everyone, especially my parents
I am not worthy of a career in psychology if I don't meet expectations

response.final_response: The pressure to nail your midterms is feeling like a huge weight on your shoulders, and it's like every mistake is being magnified as a sign that you're not enough. You've been pushing yourself so hard to meet expectations, and it's taking a toll on your mental health. Can you think of any ways that you've been able to take a step back and focus on your own goals, rather than just trying to meet someone else's standards?

turn_traces.user_message: I did try setting a timer to break up my study sessions, but every time the timer goes off I just get a sharper reminder of how much I still have to cover. I'm still stuck thinking that if I don’t get a perfect score I’m nothing. It feels like the pressure is a constant loop that drains me.

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend to help you navigate your feelings of inadequacy and pressure to perform perfectly. Given your intent to express emotion, I'll use strategies like support and complex reflection to help you process your emotions. The evidence shows that you've been struggling with feelings of not being enough and the weight of your mom's expectations, which is affecting your ability to focus on your studies. Your recent attempt to use a timer to break up your study sessions is a great effort, but it's clear that the pressure to perform is still overwhelming. My entry point will be to acknowledge the difficulty of being stuck in this constant loop of pressure and self-doubt.

evidence_used.content: I keep telling myself I have to be perfect, and it’s exhausting—like I’m running on a treadmill that never stops.
The user has been struggling with feelings of not being enough and the weight of their mom's expectations.
I am not worthy of a career in psychology if I don't meet expectations

response.final_response: You've been trying to break up your study sessions with a timer, but it's not giving you the relief you hoped for. Instead, it's just reminding you of how much you still have to cover, and that's really tough. It sounds like the pressure to perform is still feeling overwhelming, and it's hard to shake the feeling that if you don't get a perfect score, you're nothing. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when the timer goes off and you're reminded of how much you still have to do?

turn_traces.user_message: When the timer clicks, I feel the weight of all the grades I still need to get and start thinking about how my mom would judge me if I don’t hit that mark. It feels like a domino: one missed point means I’m failing not just the class but my whole identity. The anxiety spikes and I question whether I’m even good enough to keep going.

My entry point will be to acknowledge the weight of those expectations and how they're affecting the user's daily life, especially with the timer and study sessions.

I am not worthy of a career in psychology if I don't meet expectations
pressure to perform and fear of mom's judgment

response.final_response: When that timer clicks, it's like the whole weight of your mom's expectations and your own desire to prove yourself comes crashing down, and it's hard to breathe.
